topic: An adult child moves back in with their parents
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of lemon polish and old paper hung heavy in the hallway, a suffocating blanket. I kept my gaze fixed on the scuff marks on the hardwood floor, meticulously avoiding eye contact with my mother as she fussed over a vase of wilting lilies. Every small movement felt amplified under the scrutiny of their quiet, assessing presence. I tried to focus on unpacking the box of books in my room, pretending the silence of the empty space was preferable to the sudden, intense awareness of every misplaced item, every awkward angle of my posture. When my father offered a strained smile, I managed a tight nod, my throat suddenly dry, wishing the floorboards would swallow me whole.

[story 2]

The kitchen felt like a stage. I was trying to help pour coffee, a simple task, but my hands trembled slightly, the liquid sloshing precariously in the ceramic mug. I watched my sister across the counter, effortlessly flipping through a magazine, her laughter bright and unburdened. I felt a knot tighten low in my stomach, a physical pressure that made breathing shallow. "Everything alright?" my mother asked, her voice pitched just a little too brightly. I managed a weak, "Fine," the word barely escaping, immediately regretting the sound because it came out thin and reedy. I retreated to the living room, sinking onto the sofa, pulling my knees up to my chest, wishing I could become invisible.

[story 3]

The afternoon sun slanted through the window, illuminating dust motes dancing in the air, and highlighting every imperfection in my hastily arranged bookshelf. I was attempting to read, but the words swam on the page. I could feel their eyes on me, a persistent, gentle weight that made my shoulders hunch inward. When my father asked if I wanted to join them for tea, I mumbled something vague about needing to organize my things, the lie tasting metallic on my tongue. I avoided the dining room entirely, opting instead to sit by the window, tracing patterns on the glass with my fingertip, intensely aware of the stillness radiating from the room, the way my own presence seemed too loud, too noticeable.
